literal: inversion, reversal, error; from vi- + pari- + √i 'to go' (vi-pari-√i + a → viparyaya), 'a going contrary, a turning the wrong way round' — hence misapprehension, the cognition that takes a thing to be other than it is
invariant_definition: *Viparyaya* is erroneous cognition — the 'inverted' apprehension that takes a thing to be what it is not. The etymology is exact: a *going-contrary*, a turning the wrong way round, so that the cognition runs against its object. It is the standard technical term for error in several systems. In Pātañjala Yoga *viparyaya* is one of the five kinds of *vṛtti* (mental modification), defined as false knowledge not established in the real form of the thing (*mithyā-jñānam atad-rūpa-pratiṣṭham*, YS 1.8); the classic example is the silver mistakenly seen in nacre. In Sāṅkhya *viparyaya* heads the list of the four 'wrong' states (*viparyaya, aśakti, tuṣṭi, siddhi*) and, in the form of the five *viparyayas* (the traditional *tamas, moha, mahāmoha, tāmisra, andhatāmisra*), names the misidentifications that bind. In the *sthitaprajña* commentaries the term and its cognates do recurrent work: Madhusūdana classes the *kāmas* of 2.55 as *mano-vṛtti-viśeṣas* of which error (*pramāṇa-viparyaya-vikalpa-nidrā-smṛti*, the five Yoga *vṛttis*) is one species, and he repeatedly diagnoses the disturbances of the unrealized — grief, craving, the afflictions — as *viparyaya-rūpa* or *bhrānti-rūpa*, forms of inverted cognition that cannot survive in the clarified mind of the knower. *Viparyaya* thus names error not as the absence of knowledge but as a positive miscognition — a definite, content-bearing cognition that fastens on the wrong thing — and so stands beside the broader *avidyā* and the various *khyāti* (error-theories) of the schools.

school_framing.shared_core: *Viparyaya* names **inverted or erroneous cognition — the apprehension that takes a thing to be other than it is, error as a positive miscognition rather than mere want of knowledge**. The systems share this structural sense and contrast *viparyaya* with valid cognition (*pramā / yathārtha-jñāna*); they differ on its analysis — a kind of *vṛtti*, a class of binding misidentification, or the generic form of the afflictions.
school_framing.register_axes_note: **Same concept, different placement.** Yoga makes *viparyaya* one of the five *vṛttis* (YS 1.8), error defined against the real form of the object. Sāṅkhya makes it the first of the four wrong states and enumerates five binding *viparyayas*. The Gītā commentaries (Madhusūdana especially) use *viparyaya/viparīta* diagnostically — the afflictions and false hankerings of the unrealized are *viparyaya-rūpa* — connecting it to the broader analyses of error (*khyāti*) and ignorance (*avidyā*).

per_school.definition: *Viparyaya* is the second of the five *citta-vṛttis*, defined as false knowledge not founded on the real form of its object (*mithyā-jñānam atad-rūpa-pratiṣṭham*, YS 1.8) — the silver-in-nacre type of error. As a *kliṣṭa* (afflicted) *vṛtti* it is bound up with the *kleśas* and is among the modifications to be stilled in *nirodha*; the five *kleśas* are themselves identified by the tradition as the fivefold *viparyaya*.
primary_loci: Yoga-Bhāṣya on 1.8 (viparyaya and the five kleśas)

per_school.definition: *Viparyaya* is the first of the four wrong conditions of the intellect (*viparyaya, aśakti, tuṣṭi, siddhi*, SK 46-51) and is elaborated as the five *viparyayas* — the traditional *tamas, moha, mahāmoha, tāmisra, andhatāmisra* — the misidentifications by which the *puruṣa* is taken to be what it is not and bondage is sustained. Error here is the principal obstacle that discriminative knowledge (*viveka*) removes.

Madhusūdana classes the *kāmas* of 2.55 as mental modifications of which *viparyaya* (error) is one of the five Yoga-*vṛtti* species (*pramāṇa-viparyaya-vikalpa-nidrā-smṛti*), and throughout the discourse he diagnoses the disturbances of the unrealized — grief, vain hankering, the afflictions — as *viparyaya-rūpa* / *bhrānti-rūpa*, inverted cognitions that cannot arise in the clarified mind (*śuddha-sattva*) of the *sthitaprajña*. Error, so understood, is removed not by suppression but by the knowledge of the real that supplants it.
primary_loci: Gūḍhārtha-dīpikā (Madhusūdana) on 2.56-57 (the afflictions as viparyaya-rūpa / bhrānti-rūpa)

translator_note: Render *viparyaya* as 'erroneous cognition', 'misapprehension', or 'inversion', keeping the literal sense of a *turning-contrary* — the cognition runs the wrong way against its object. Do not render it merely as 'ignorance': *viparyaya* is a positive, content-bearing miscognition (taking nacre to be silver), not the bare absence of knowledge, and the schools distinguish it from *avidyā* even while relating the two. In Yoga it is a specific *vṛtti*; in Sāṅkhya a specific class of binding states; do not flatten these technical placements into a generic 'error' where the precision matters.
